column2: Unit 4 Quiz: Writing
column3: Test your understanding of all 7 lessons from Unit 4 — Writing!

column2: What is the PURPOSE of opinion writing?
column10: The purpose of opinion writing is to share your view AND convince the reader to agree with you by giving strong reasons and details. Unlike informational writing (which just informs), opinion writing is persuasive -- you are trying to change or influence what the reader thinks.

column2: To entertain the reader with an exciting made-up story
To explain a topic using only facts and definitions
To share your view and convince the reader with reasons and details
To describe a real event in the order it happened
Which sentence is the BEST opinion statement for a paragraph?
column10: 'I believe that students should have at least 30 minutes of free reading time every day' is the best opinion statement because it clearly states a specific view in a complete sentence. 'Reading is good' is too vague. 'Books have pages and words' is a fact, not an opinion. 'Some people like reading' doesn't state the writer's view.

column2: Reading is good.
I believe that students should have at least 30 minutes of free reading time every day.
Books have pages and words in them.
Some people like reading and some people don't.
Which linking word would BEST connect a second reason in an opinion paragraph?
column10: 'Furthermore' is a linking word used to introduce an additional reason, making it perfect for connecting a second or third reason in an opinion paragraph. 'However' is used for contrast. 'For example' introduces examples. 'In conclusion' signals the end of the piece.

column2: However
For example
Furthermore
In conclusion
What is the main difference between OPINION writing and INFORMATIONAL writing?
column10: Opinion writing shares the writer's personal view and tries to persuade the reader. Informational writing shares facts and explains a topic without personal opinions -- the writer's own views do not appear. In informational writing, you would not use phrases like 'I believe' or 'I think'.

column2: Opinion writing uses longer sentences than informational writing
Opinion writing tries to persuade; informational writing explains facts without personal views
Informational writing always uses dialogue; opinion writing never does
They are the same type of writing with different names
In narrative writing, what is the CONFLICT?
column10: The conflict is the problem or challenge that the main character faces in a story. It is what creates tension and keeps the reader engaged. Without a conflict, nothing interesting happens in the story! The conflict appears in the middle section and is resolved at the end.

column2: The setting where the story takes place
The description of what the main character looks like
The problem or challenge that the main character faces
The first sentence of the story that introduces the characters
Which sentence uses the 'SHOW, DON'T TELL' technique effectively?
column10: 'Her hands shook and she pressed herself against the wall, hardly daring to breathe' SHOWS fear through specific physical actions and details -- the reader feels it without being told. 'She was scared' just TELLS us the emotion without any vivid detail. Showing through specific actions and sensory details is a hallmark of strong narrative writing.

column2: She was scared.
He felt happy about the news.
Her hands shook and she pressed herself against the wall, hardly daring to breathe.
The weather was bad outside.
What are TEMPORAL WORDS used for in writing?
column10: Temporal words (like first, next, then, finally, meanwhile, eventually) signal time and order in writing. They tell the reader WHEN things happen and in what sequence, making the writing clear and easy to follow. Without temporal words, a sequence of events can feel jumbled and confusing.

column2: To describe what a character looks like in great detail
To signal time and order, showing when events happen in sequence
To convince the reader that your opinion is correct
To connect an opinion with reasons in persuasive writing
What is the difference between REVISING and EDITING?
column10: Revising means going back to improve your IDEAS -- adding details, reordering events, strengthening reasons, removing things that don't fit. Editing means fixing MISTAKES -- correcting spelling, punctuation, capitalisation, and grammar errors. Revising comes before editing in the writing process.

column2: Revising fixes spelling mistakes; editing improves your ideas
Revising and editing are exactly the same step in the writing process
Revising improves your ideas; editing fixes mistakes in spelling and punctuation
Revising is done by a teacher; editing is done by the student
What comes FIRST in the writing process?
column10: Prewriting (planning) comes first in the writing process. Before you write a single sentence, you should brainstorm ideas, organise your thoughts using a graphic organiser, and decide on your topic, audience, and purpose. Planning makes the drafting stage much easier and helps produce better writing.

column2: Editing -- fix all mistakes before you start
Publishing -- create the final version first
Prewriting -- plan and organise your ideas before writing
Revising -- improve your ideas from the very beginning
In opinion writing, the concluding statement should introduce a completely new idea not mentioned earlier in the piece.
column10: False! The concluding statement should RESTATE the opinion in a new way and wrap up the piece -- it should NOT introduce brand new ideas. A good conclusion reminds the reader of your opinion and gives a sense of closure. Phrases like 'For these reasons,' 'That is why,' and 'In conclusion' are great for concluding statements.

column2: True
False
When writing dialogue, each time a new character speaks, you should start a new line.
column10: True! This is one of the most important rules for writing dialogue. Starting a new line for each new speaker makes it clear WHO is talking and prevents confusion. Without this rule, readers would lose track of which character is saying what, making the dialogue very hard to follow.

column2: True
False
The word 'finally' is an example of a temporal word that signals the END of a sequence of events.
column10: True! 'Finally' is a temporal word that signals the last event in a sequence. Other temporal words for endings include 'at last,' 'in the end,' 'eventually,' and 'after a while.' Temporal words for the beginning include 'first' and 'to begin,' and for the middle include 'next,' 'then,' and 'meanwhile.'

column2: True
False
The exact words spoken by a character in a story, placed inside quotation marks, are called {dash}.
column10: The exact words spoken by characters are called DIALOGUE. Dialogue is one of the most powerful tools in narrative writing -- it reveals character personality, creates tension, and makes stories feel alive and real. Always put dialogue inside quotation marks and start a new line for each new speaker.

column2: dialogue
The stage of the writing process where you fix spelling, punctuation, and grammar mistakes is called {dash}.
column10: This stage is called EDITING. Editing is different from revising -- revising improves your ideas, while editing corrects errors. Use an editing checklist to make sure every sentence starts with a capital letter, every sentence ends with correct punctuation, all proper nouns are capitalised, and spelling has been checked.

column2: editing

Write a short opinion paragraph (4-6 sentences) about whether schools should have more art classes. Include: a clear opinion statement, at least one reason with a detail, a linking word, and a concluding statement.
column10: A strong answer will include: (1) a clear opinion statement using 'I believe,' 'I think,' or 'In my opinion'; (2) at least one specific reason with a supporting detail (e.g., 'Art classes help students develop creativity, which is important in every subject'); (3) at least one linking word (First, Furthermore, Because, For these reasons, etc.); and (4) a concluding statement that restates the opinion. Award credit for any well-structured, clearly expressed opinion paragraph that follows these four elements.
